prescription_options: Topical retinoid (tretinoin 0.025–0.05%) or topical azelaic acid 15–20% if PIH is a concern.
Dermatologist evaluation for possible topical antibiotic + BPO combo, hormonal workup, or oral antibiotics.
Topical retinoid + topical antibiotic (clindamycin) with BPO. Or topical dapsone 5% gel for adult females.
Oral antibiotics (doxycycline 50–100 mg daily or sarecycline) for 3–4 months max, always with topical retinoid.
Hormonal therapy for females (spironolactone 50–200 mg/day or combined oral contraceptives).
Dermatologist referral for isotretinoin consideration if nodulocystic, scarring, or treatment-resistant.
Immediate dermatologist referral. Oral isotretinoin 0.5–1 mg/kg/day is first-line for severe nodulocystic acne.
Intralesional corticosteroid injections (triamcinolone 2.5–5 mg/mL) for large nodules/cysts.
procedure_options: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) or blue/red light therapy as adjunct.

prescription_options: Topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus 0.03–0.1% or pimecrolimus 1%) for face/folds and maintenance.
Crisaborole 2% ointment (Eucrisa) or ruxolitinib 1.5% cream for mild-moderate AD.
Topical calcineurin inhibitors for face/folds. Wet wrap therapy for severe flares.
Phototherapy (NB-UVB) 2–3x weekly for widespread disease.
Dupilumab (Dupixent) biweekly injection — first-line biologic for moderate-severe AD.
Urgent dermatologist referral. Dupilumab or tralokinumab biologic therapy.
Oral JAK inhibitors (upadacitinib or abrocitinib) for severe refractory AD.
procedure_options: Phototherapy (NB-UVB or UVA1). Wet wrap therapy for acute flares.

prescription_options: Metronidazole 0.75–1% cream/gel or azelaic acid 15% gel for papulopustular rosacea.
Topical ivermectin 1% cream (Soolantra) for papules/pustules. Or topical metronidazole + azelaic acid.
Low-dose oral doxycycline 40 mg (Oracea) — anti-inflammatory sub-antimicrobial dose for papulopustular rosacea.
Brimonidine 0.33% gel (Mirvaso) or oxymetazoline 1% cream (Rhofade) for persistent facial erythema.
Dermatologist referral for severe papulopustular or ocular rosacea. Oral isotretinoin may be considered (low dose).
procedure_options: Vascular laser (pulsed dye laser 585–595 nm or KTP laser) for persistent erythema and telangiectasias.

prescription_options: Azelaic acid 20% cream or hydroquinone 2–4% for 8–12 weeks with strict photoprotection.
Triple combination cream (hydroquinone 4% + tretinoin 0.05% + mild steroid) for 8 weeks.
Add oral tranexamic acid 250 mg BID (off-label) for refractory melasma, after thrombosis screening.
Cysteamine 5% cream (Cyspera) — non-hydroquinone option for maintenance.
Dermatologist referral. Oral tranexamic acid + topical triple combination or azelaic acid 20%.
procedure_options: Very conservative chemical peels (low-strength glycolic or salicylic acid series) ONLY by experienced provider. Test spots mandatory.
Low-fluence Q-switched Nd:YAG 1064 nm or fractional laser ONLY as last resort by melasma specialist. High PIH risk.

prescription_options: Hydroquinone 2–4% for 8–12 weeks OR azelaic acid 20% for longer-term maintenance.
HQ 4% + tretinoin 0.025–0.05% combination. Use at night with strict daytime SPF.
Add oral tranexamic acid 250 mg BID (after thrombosis screening) for refractory PIH.
procedure_options: Conservative chemical peel series (salicylic acid 20–30% or mandelic acid 20–40%). Multiple sessions.

prescription_options: Topical corticosteroids (betamethasone dipropionate) + vitamin D analogs (calcipotriene) for body plaques.
Phototherapy (NB-UVB) 2–3x weekly. First-line for moderate widespread psoriasis.
Systemic non-biologics: methotrexate 7.5–25 mg weekly OR apremilast (Otezla) OR cyclosporine short-term.
Biologic agents: TNF inhibitors (adalimumab, etanercept) or IL-17 inhibitors (secukinumab, ixekizumab).
Urgent dermatology referral. Biologic agents: IL-23 inhibitors (guselkumab, risankizumab) or IL-17 inhibitors.
Combination therapy: biologic + methotrexate (enhances efficacy, reduces immunogenicity).
procedure_options: Phototherapy (NB-UVB or PUVA) as adjunct. Targeted excimer laser for resistant plaques.

prescription_options: Ketoconazole 2% cream or ciclopirox 0.77% gel for facial/seborrheic areas.
Ketoconazole 2% shampoo/cream + low-potency corticosteroid (hydrocortisone 1–2.5%) for inflammation.
Topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus 0.1% or pimecrolimus 1%) for steroid-sparing maintenance, especially on face.
Oral antifungal (itraconazole 200 mg daily for 7 days) for severe or refractory cases.
Topical retinoid (tretinoin 0.025% or adapalene 0.1%) for persistent cases.
Topical metronidazole 0.75% gel or azelaic acid 15–20% for papules.
Low-dose oral tetracycline (doxycycline 40–100 mg/day or minocycline 50–100 mg/day) for 6–12 weeks.
Low- to medium-potency topical corticosteroid (hydrocortisone 1–2.5% or triamcinolone 0.1%) for 1–2 weeks.
Patch testing by dermatologist for allergic contact dermatitis. Refer if persistent or recurrent.
Medium-potency topical steroid (triamcinolone 0.1% or betamethasone valerate 0.1%) for 1–2 weeks.
Topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus 0.1% or pimecrolimus 1%) for face, eyelids, or maintenance.
Oral corticosteroid short course (prednisone taper) for widespread allergic contact dermatitis.
